name: Over Wallop
overview: Over Wallop is the westernmost of The Wallops — three contiguous villages strung along the chalk-stream Wallop Brook — and the source of the Brook itself, which rises in the parish and flows south-east to join the River Test below Bossington. The village sits in the Test Valley district immediately adjacent to the Wiltshire border, approximately five miles north-west of Stockbridge and seven miles south-west of Andover. It is named in Domesday as 'the other Wallop' — distinguishing it from Nether Wallop, the larger of the original pair. The 12th-century parish church of St Peter (Diocese of Winchester) anchors the southern end of the village; Victorian restorations are visible. Archaeology runs deep: flint mines in the parish date to roughly 3,983 BC, and the Quarley High Linear earthwork to around 245 BC. The Wallops share two pubs, a village hall, a Good village primary at Nether Wallop, a veterinary surgery and a shop with post office. Over Wallop has its own Conservation Area designation and an active Neighbourhood Development Plan (adopted 2022) focused on resisting excessive housing development.
character: Loose linear village along chalk lanes with timber-framed and brick-and-flint cottages, plus a number of 20th-century additions. Working agricultural village rather than show village; population around 1,000 across the parish. The Brook frames the conservation area and is the social spine.
amenities: The White Hart (Over Wallop village pub)
Wallops shop with Post Office (Nether Wallop)

schools.name: Test Valley School / Danebury School, Stockbridge

commutes.mode: drive + train (SWR West of England line)

transport.roads: A343 ~1 mi; A30 (Stockbridge) ~5 mi; A303 ~3 mi

thingsToDo: Walking the Wallop Brook Way (4.7-mile route)
Army Flying Museum (1.5 mi at Middle Wallop)
pros: Wallop Primary (Good) shared across all three Wallops villages
Quietest of the Wallops — furthest from the A343 and AAC airfield noise
Grateley station 3 mi for Waterloo trains via SWR West of England line
Strong neighbourhood plan protecting rural character
cons: Wiltshire-border position means some council services are split with Wiltshire CC
Secondary catchment school rated Inadequate in 2022
AAC airfield helicopter noise audible across the parish
Limited evening transport; pub closure or shop turnover hurts daily life
whoItSuits: Buyers wanting the chalk-stream Test Valley character at slightly lower prices than Stockbridge proper, with a Good village primary and decent train access via either Grateley or Andover. Quieter than Middle Wallop because further from the airfield, but the helicopters are still audible. Best fit for families with primary-age children, service-family households on Middle Wallop posting wanting some buffer from the airfield, and downsizers from Stockbridge or Salisbury. The Wiltshire-border position means some practical services (utility providers, county-specific schemes) need cross-county checks.
primarySupermarket: Waitrose, Stockbridge (5.0 mi); Sainsbury's, Andover (7.0 mi)
